frames.caption: Sort by start (already sorted here), then seed current = intervals[0] = [1,3].
frames.note: The whole algorithm is "sort once, sweep once". After sorting by start the timeline runs left to right, so we can carry a single running interval `current` and decide each next one with one comparison. The sort is the dominant cost at O(n log n); the sweep that follows is a single O(n) pass.

frames.caption: i = 1: next = intervals[1] = [2,6]. Compare next.start 2 with current.end 3.
frames.note: We test the next interval against the running end. After a start-sort, the only way `next` can join the current block is by starting at or before current.end — so a single comparison decides overlap-vs-gap. This is the line that the whole sweep pivots on.

frames.caption: Overlap: 2 ≤ 3. Extend current.end to Math.Max(3, 6) = 6 → current = [1,6].
frames.note: next.start ≤ current.end, so the two ranges touch or overlap — they belong to one merged block. We widen current by taking Math.Max(current.end, next.end), NEVER blindly next.end: a short interval fully inside a long one (e.g. [1,9] then [2,3]) must keep the 9. Because the array is sorted by start, next.start is already ≥ current.start, so this single comparison against the running end is all the overlap test we need.

frames.caption: i = 2: next = intervals[2] = [8,10]. Compare next.start 8 with current.end 6.
frames.note: We test the next interval against the running end. After a start-sort, the only way `next` can join the current block is by starting at or before current.end — so a single comparison decides overlap-vs-gap. This is the line that the whole sweep pivots on.

frames.caption: Gap: 8 > current.end. Push current to merged, then start a new run current = [8,10].
frames.note: next.start > current.end, so a true gap separates them — nothing later can reach back across it (starts only increase from here). That means current is final: push it to the output and reset current to next, opening a new run. Sorting by start is exactly what guarantees one forward pass suffices: an interval that overlaps the run must begin within or at the current end.

frames.caption: i = 3: next = intervals[3] = [15,18]. Compare next.start 15 with current.end 10.
frames.note: We test the next interval against the running end. After a start-sort, the only way `next` can join the current block is by starting at or before current.end — so a single comparison decides overlap-vs-gap. This is the line that the whole sweep pivots on.

frames.caption: Gap: 15 > current.end. Push current to merged, then start a new run current = [15,18].
frames.note: next.start > current.end, so a true gap separates them — nothing later can reach back across it (starts only increase from here). That means current is final: push it to the output and reset current to next, opening a new run. Sorting by start is exactly what guarantees one forward pass suffices: an interval that overlaps the run must begin within or at the current end.

frames.caption: Loop ends — flush the final current [15,18] to merged. Result: [1,6] [8,10] [15,18].
frames.note: The loop only pushes when a gap appears, so the last run never triggers a push from inside the loop — it must be flushed after. Forgetting this single line silently drops the final interval, one of the classic merge-intervals bugs. Total cost is O(n log n) (the sort) plus an O(n) sweep, with O(n) output space.
